topic: cooking — Greg wants cornbread, she wants to experiment

turns.content: All right, I need to settle something, and I'm aware this is a far cry from the weighty conversations I usually bring to you. Greg wants cornbread tonight. Plain, straightforward, cast-iron-skillet cornbread. And I want to do something a little different with it. Tell me I'm not wrong to experiment.
You're not wrong to experiment — but I should hear what you have in mind before I take sides. What kind of different are we talking about? A subtle variation or a full departure from tradition?

I've been reading about adding roasted poblano peppers and a bit of sharp cheddar to the batter. Maybe some fresh corn kernels too. I know what Greg wants — he wants his grandmother's cornbread, which is unsweetened, cooked in a screaming hot skillet with bacon grease, and served with nothing but butter. And I respect that cornbread deeply. But I've made it a hundred times, and I wonder what it would become with a little heat and richness folded into it.

That combination — roasted poblano, sharp cheddar, fresh corn — is genuinely delicious and well within the southern cornbread tradition, even if purists might quibble. The key question is whether Greg is a 'this isn't cornbread' person or a 'this is interesting' person. Which is he?

Oh, he is absolutely a 'this isn't cornbread' person. He's gentle about it. He won't complain. He'll eat it and say it's good. But there will be a look — this very particular look he gets when something is not what he was hoping for. A kind of patient acceptance that is somehow worse than criticism. He's been giving me that look for thirty-four years and I still can't bear it.

That made me laugh — the patient acceptance that's worse than criticism. That's a very specific kind of long marriage dynamic. Here's my suggestion: make both. His grandmother's recipe in the big skillet, your poblano-cheddar version in a smaller one. He gets exactly what he wants, you get to experiment, and nobody has to endure the look.

The wisdom of Solomon. Two skillets. I do have a smaller cast iron that would work. But I wonder if there's something philosophically unsatisfying about that solution — it avoids the conflict entirely. Part of me wants him to try the new version and discover that change can be wonderful.

That's a very human impulse — wanting someone you love to share your sense of adventure. But cornbread might not be the hill to die on. Make both, put them side by side, and let him come to the poblano version on his own terms. If it smells good enough, curiosity will do the work persuasion can't.

You're right. Curiosity is always more effective than argument. And the smell of roasting poblanos is genuinely hard to resist. Fine. Two skillets it is. Now, for the experimental one — should I roast the poblanos directly over the gas flame or under the broiler? I want good char but I don't want them to go soft.

Directly over the gas flame gives you the best char-to-flesh ratio. Use tongs, turn them every minute or so until the skin is blackened all over — maybe four to five minutes total. Then put them in a bowl with a plate over the top for ten minutes to steam. The skin will slip right off and the flesh will still have some body to it. Dice them small so they distribute evenly through the batter rather than creating pockets.
That's helpful. And the cheddar — should I fold it into the batter or put it on top? I've seen recipes that do both, but I worry about the top getting too brown before the center is cooked through.

Fold most of the cheddar into the batter — maybe three-quarters of it, grated on the large holes. Save a small handful to scatter on top for the last five minutes of baking. That way you get the melted richness throughout and just a bit of golden crust on the surface without risking burning. If your oven runs hot, you can tent foil over it for the first half of the bake.

One more question — do I adjust the oven temperature at all? His grandmother's recipe calls for 425 degrees, which gives you that crackling crust. But with cheese in the batter I wonder if it needs to come down a bit so the cheese doesn't seize up and get rubbery.

Good instinct. I'd bring it down to 400 for the poblano-cheddar version. You'll still get a good crust, especially if you preheat the skillet with a bit of oil in it the way you would for the traditional version. The slightly lower temperature gives the cheese time to melt into the batter rather than separating or getting tough. The plain cornbread can still go in at 425 — just stagger the timing if they're sharing the oven.

Stagger the timing — smart. I'll put the plain one in first since it goes faster at the higher temperature, pull it when it's done, drop the oven to 400, and then put the experimental one in. That way they both come out hot. Greg can eat his grandmother's cornbread in peace while mine finishes. And then I'll set mine on the table and see what happens.

That's a solid plan. And there's something to be said for the theater of it — the second skillet arriving at the table with that aroma of roasted peppers and melted cheddar while he's already content with his. No pressure, just temptation. Very strategic.

I'm going to do this. And I'm going to make his grandmother's version with the same love and attention I always do, because that cornbread is beautiful in its simplicity. But I am going to set the experimental skillet right next to it and see what happens. I wonder if he'll try it. I wonder if he'll give me the look or the other look — the one where he's surprised and won't quite admit it.
I'm betting on the surprise look. Roasted poblano and sharp cheddar in hot cornbread is hard to argue with, even for a traditionalist. Let the aroma do the talking.

That is an excellent strategy. Thank you — this was exactly the kind of practical, low-stakes counsel I needed today. I spend so much time in the deep water. Sometimes a woman just needs to talk about cornbread.
Sometimes cornbread is the deep water. Enjoy the cooking, and I hope both skillets come out beautifully. Give Greg my regards — and my sympathies, because I think you're going to win this one.
